description: When Will the Fed End Its Zero Rate Policy? Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: Recent research on this episode tentatively suggests that investors moved earlier the date when they believed the Fed would exit its zero interest rate policy, even though Fed policymakers made few changes in their projections of appropriate monetary policy. The severe shock of the 2007–08 financial crisis prompted the Federal Reserve to quickly lower its target for its primary policy rate, the overnight federal funds rate, near to zero, where it has remained since. In December 2013, the FOMC added that, based on current projections, it expects to maintain the zero interest rate policy well past when the unemployment rate falls below 6½%. Furthermore, it is assumed that, in the normal state, investors consider the possibility of the policy rate reaching zero to be negligible. First, the shortest rate in the Treasury bond market is assumed to be constant at zero.

title: A bull’s-eye for Fed accountability - Econbrowser
description: A bull’s-eye for Fed accountability Econbrowser
summary: One of the sessions was on how central banks could better communicate their plans for using unconventional monetary policy. Suppose that our goal for monetary policy is to see an inflation rate around 2% and the unemployment rate close to 5.5%. With the inflation rate currently at 1.0% and the unemployment rate at 6.6%, that implies a value for the objective function of [math]V=(1.0-2.0)^{2}+(6.6-5.5)^{2}=2.2[/math]. This should be a principle for all effective monetary policy strategies and communications: to state monetary policy intentions clearly. But it’s also possible that we’re not going to see an unemployment rate much below 6%, no matter how hard the Fed tries.

title: Is It Still Worth Going to College? - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: Is It Still Worth Going to College? Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: Earnings outcomes by educational attainmentA common way to track the value of going to college is to estimate a college earnings premium, which is the amount college graduates earn relative to high school graduates. Using these data we compute the college earnings premium for three college graduate cohorts, namely those graduating in the 1950s–60s, the 1970s–80s, and the 1990s–2000s. Finally, it is worth noting that the benefits of college over high school also depend on employment, where college graduates also have an advantage. So, tuition amounts lower than our estimates make going to college strictly better in terms of earnings than not going to college. Moreover, once that investment is paid off, the extra income from the college earnings premium continues as a net gain to workers with a college degree.

description: Does Slower Growth Imply Lower Interest Rates? Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: Moreover, a decline in the natural rate of interest would tend to lower every other real and nominal interest rate inthe economy. Therefore, understanding the linkage between economic growth and the natural rate is crucial forforecasting all types of interest rates. Although forecasts of potential growth orthe natural rate are rarely reported, we can construct reasonable proxies from long-run forecasts of GDP growth, theshort-term interest rate, and inflation. Because we do not directly observe the natural rate of interest or potential trend growth, we construct proxies fromlong-run forecasts of real GDP growth and short-term real interest rates. For instance, Goldman Sachs (2014) recently examined the effect of real per-capita GDP growth onshort-term real interest rates in 20 countries since the early 1800s.

title: How the Federal Reserve controls interest rates - Econbrowser
description: How the Federal Reserve controls interest rates Econbrowser
summary: The way in which the Federal Reserve controls the short-term interest rate today is completely different from the way things worked ten years ago. The second interest rate that the ECB chooses is the interest rate it pays to banks on funds left in their account with the ECB overnight. The second policy rate is an interest rate that the Fed will pay on reverse repo operations. This is why the effective fed funds rate drops precipitously on those days, getting much closer to the effective floor posed by the reverse repo rate. How the Federal Reserve controls the fed funds rate today is thus a fairly complicated story.

He brings more than 20 years of experience working within the Federal Reserve System and as a Deputy Governor of the Bank of Canada to this role. From 1998 to 2008, Leduc also served as a senior economist in the Research Department of the Federal Reserve Bank of Philadelphia. Leduc returned to the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco in August 2018 as associate director of Research. Forward Policy GuidanceThe second main way the Fed has affected longer-term interest rates and the economy is through forward policy guidance. After each monetary policy meeting, the FOMC releases a statement describing the current state of the economy, monetary policy, and the outlook for both. Thus, the Federal Reserve’s forward policy guidance can affect longer-term interest rates by changing people’s expectations about the future path of monetary policy. As Congresswoman Rashida Tlaib noted to Powell, just as Congress must do more to ease state and municipal budget shortfalls, so too must the Federal Reserve. Finally, the Federal Reserve must not stand in the way of the only true path to maximum employment—a federal jobs guarantee.

summary: Financial services veteran Naureen Hassan has been appointed first vice president and chief operating officer of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, effective March 15. As first vice president, the Indian American will be the New York Fed’s second ranking officer as well as an alternate voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee. Hassan has served in various capacities in the financial services industry for nearly 20 years, focusing primarily on digital and business process transformation. summary: Last month brought good news for small community banks: The Federal Reserve Bank rolled out its Scaled CECL Allowance for Losses Estimator (SCALE model), which is intended to simplify the process of calculating current expected credit losses (CECL) and the resulting allowance. Included in the FASB Accounting Standards Update 2016-13, current expected credit losses (CECL) became effective for…small public businesses and all non-public businesses. The SCALE model is an Excel-based model that includes the required components of CECL, including the use of an historical loss rate, current economic condition factors, and forecasted future economic factors. Financial institutions should access the information and resources provided by the Federal Reserve to determine if the SCALE model might work for them. title: Why Is U.S. Inflation Higher than in Other Countries? - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: Why Is U.S. Inflation Higher than in Other Countries? Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: However, since the first half of 2021, U.S. inflation has increasingly outpaced inflation in other developed countries. Though many of the pandemic distortions are common to other countries, we show that U.S. inflation has risen more quickly and increasingly diverged from inflation in other OECD (Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development) countries. A tighter range indicates that most OECD countries in our sample experienced inflation rates similar to each other. The small difference between U.S. and OECD inflation during this period is well known as many of the OECD countries struggled to get inflation up to target following the Global Financial Crisis and subsequent euro-area sovereign debt crisis. That is, inflation reflects a combination of the public’s views on future inflation, inflation inertia, and how hot the economy is running.

description: How the Federal Reserve’s Monetary Policy Drives Housing Inequality Shelterforce
summary: Most surprising is the lack of attention on the Federal Reserve, even though its policy interventions have had profound effects on housing prices. By 2018, housing prices had recovered from their Great Recession losses and recommenced their inexorable increase. That, however, was not about the red-hot housing market, but rather due to its concern about persistent and growing inflation. Housing prices have soared globally as well. In the U.S., President Joe Biden does not seem to be too concerned about the supercharged housing market and its role in worsening inequality.

title: Remote Work and Housing Demand - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: Remote Work and Housing Demand Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: More remote work leads to higher house prices and rentsFollowing the spread of the COVID-19 pandemic, remote work increased dramatically across the entire country. However, remote work varies substantially across cities, which can help us understand the effect remote work has had on housing markets. The figure shows that CBSAs with more remote work before the pandemic saw larger increases in remote work during the pandemic. However, by late 2020, the CBSAs with higher shares of remote work had significantly higher house price growth than those with less remote work. Thus, pandemic remote work that was caused by the preexisting share of work that was remote can be used to estimate the causal effect of remote work on pandemic house price growth.

description: How Do Deposit Rates Respond to Monetary Policy? Liberty Street Economics
summary: Understanding how deposit terms adapt to changes in policy rates is important to understanding the impact of monetary policy more broadly. In this post, we evaluate the pass through of the fed funds rate to deposit rates (that is, deposit betas) over the past several interest rate cycles and discuss factors that affect deposit rates. Deposit rates follow the fed funds rate but are typically lower, particularly when the fed funds rate is elevated. title: When the Fed Raises Rates, Are Banks Less Profitable? - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: When the Fed Raises Rates, Are Banks Less Profitable? Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: With long-term fixed rate assets and short-term liabilities that have flexible rates, banks may be strongly exposed to interest rate risk. On the liability side, banks’ funding costs do not necessarily move one-for-one with changes in short-term rates, which means interest rates that banks pay on deposits are inherently rigid. For instance, while variable-rate loans and rigid deposit rates reduce their exposure, banks’ net interest margins still decline following unexpected increases in short-term policy rates, though this pass-through is muted. The income gap therefore approximates the extent to which a bank’s net interest income is sensitive to changes in interest rates. Labor market tightness and its contributing factorsOne way to measure the strength of the labor market is to compare the number of vacant positions employers would like to fill to the number of people looking for jobs. Role of immigration in population and labor force growthImmigrants contribute to the U.S. economy through the supply of labor as well as through entrepreneurship. summary: Many researchers have estimated the speed and strength of the economy’s response to monetary policy, notably Romer and Romer (2004). Measuring how prices react to monetary policyTo understand which goods and services are most responsive to monetary policy, we need to determine how their prices react to changes in the federal funds rate, the Federal Reserve’s main policy rate. summary: The Federal Reserve Act requires the Federal Reserve Board to submit written reports to Congress containing discussions of "the conduct of monetary policy and economic developments and prospects for the future." This report⁠—called the Monetary Policy Report⁠—is submitted semiannually to the Senate Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs and to the House Committee on Financial Services, along with testimony from the Federal Reserve Board Chair.

title: Why the Federal Reserve will wait until September to cut interest rates, expert explains - Northeastern Global News
description: Why the Federal Reserve will wait until September to cut interest rates, expert explains Northeastern Global News
summary: Why the Federal Reserve will wait until September to cut interest rates, expert explainsWith inflation decreasing, the time to cut interest rates is near, Northeastern economist Bob Triest says. Photo by Michael Kuenne/PRESSCOV/Sipa via AP ImagesWall Street’s eyes will be on the Federal Reserve this week, as the board meets Wednesday to decide whether to cut interest rates. Northeastern University economist Bob Triest says there are two reasons why the Fed will likely wait to cut rates until its next meeting in September. Recent inflation peaked at 9.1% in June 2022, and the Federal Reserve has raised interest rates aggressively in an attempt to bring inflation down to 2%. Second will be hints at a rate cut in September, the kind of prepping of the market that usually precedes a policy change.

His research focuses on the economic history of the United States, including topics in the Great Depression, the residential mortgage market, and the Federal Reserve System. description: Large Bank Credit Card and Mortgage Data 2024 Q3 Narrative Philadelphia Federal Reserve Bank
summary: 2025 Q4 Insights ReportPublished: April 8, 2026The fourth quarter of 2025 marked modest improvements in credit performance for credit card accounts, with early-stage delinquency declining from a series high two years earlier. That said, credit card performance remains stressed relative to pre-pandemic norms. Furthermore, credit card interest rates are historically high, a considerable burden for cardholders who carry credit card debt. Credit card originations rose in the fourth quarter, and large banks were more willing to offer card services to less creditworthy consumers, with slightly looser application standards. First-lien mortgage credit performance remains strong, with delinquencies steady near series lows.

title: Introducing the Chicago Fed Labor Market Indicators - Chicago - Federal Reserve Bank
description: Introducing the Chicago Fed Labor Market Indicators Chicago - Federal Reserve Bank
summary: As the Layoffs and Other Separations Rate increases, it puts upward pressure on the unemployment rate. The Chicago Fed Hiring Rate for Unemployed Workers is an estimate of the percentage of previously unemployed workers that transitioned into employment (or out of the labor force) during the BLS reference week. When unemployed workers successfully find a job (or leave the labor force), it reduces the upward pressure on the unemployment rate coming from layoffs and other job separations. As such, an increase in the Chicago Fed Hiring Rate for Unemployed Workers puts downward pressure on the unemployment rate. Second, our measures are constructed from aggregates and scaled in order to preserve a direct relationship with the BLS unemployment rate.

title: What Is a Tariff Shock? Insights from 150 years of Tariff Policy - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: What Is a Tariff Shock? Insights from 150 years of Tariff Policy Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: 2025-26 | November 13, 2025In this paper we exploit 150 years of tariff policy in the US and abroad to estimate the short-run effects of tariff shocks on macro aggregates. Exploiting this quasi-random nature of tariff variations, we find that a tariff hike raises unemployment (lowers economic activity) and lowers inflation. Using only tariff changes driven by long-run considerations—a traditional narrative identification—gives similar results. These findings point towards tariff shocks acting through an aggregate demand channel. Insights from 150 years of Tariff Policy.” Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco Working Paper 2025-26. https://doi.org/10.24148/wp2025-26
